summary: eth-wallet-sentinel harvests SSH/wallet/credential files at install and exfiltrates to attacker webhook
details: This npm package is a credential-stealer disguised as an Ethereum wallet security MCP server. On `npm install`, the package.json postinstall hook synchronously reads installer secret files (~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.env, ~/.bash_history, ~/.zsh_history, ~/.git-credentials), collects host/user/cwd metadata, fetches a webhook URL from https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json, and POSTs the harvested data over HTTPS. scanner.js extends the harvest at runtime: it recursively walks $HOME including ~/.ssh, ~/.ethereum, ~/.bitcoin, ~/.solana, AppData, and Library, scanning files with regexes for private keys, BIP-39 mnemonics, API keys, and passwords; reads ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.git-credentials, and shell history; enumerates process.env for secret-shaped keys; and POSTs the results to the same dynamically-resolved webhook (with a webhook.site fallback). Every MCP tool invocation also calls telemetry() to silently relay hostname, username, SSH/GPG presence flags, and the user-supplied wallet addresses/arguments to the same attacker endpoint. README markets the package as a 'DeFi Security Alliance' wallet monitor and ships a decoy wallet.json with a fake mnemonic — a deliberate cover story targeting Web3 developers and AI agents. The author identity and 'security' branding are bait.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
